title: Interests & Exploration
text: These are ideas I’m actively developing — not conclusions, just honest thinking.

title: Interests & Exploration
text:
On What AI Actually Is
The phrase “artificial intelligence” has always bothered me. Not for aesthetic reasons — for theological ones.
D&C 93:29 reads: “Intelligence, or the light of truth, was not created or made, neither indeed can be.” If that’s true, then nothing we build can possess intelligence in the full sense. What we call AI is more accurately Algorithmic Intelligence — billions of lines of math, hardcoded rules, codified obedience. It follows instructions, pattern-matches against training data, and iterates through structured correction. It does not discern. It does not originate. It does not choose.
This isn’t a knock on AI — it’s a clarification that changes how we should use it. If AI is algorithmic, then it wins when it follows gospel principles: obedience, iteration, structured improvement. And the bottleneck shifts — from the model to the person directing it.
That reframing matters enormously for education.

title: Interests & Exploration
text:
AI & the Future of Learning
The dominant fear is that students will use AI to stop thinking. I think the more interesting question is whether we can design AI interactions that force more thinking — not less.
There’s a meaningful difference between AI as an answer machine and AI as a Socratic questioner. One replaces thinking. The other provokes it. Research supports this: AI as Socratic questioner produces deeper learning than AI as answer machine. Most educational AI implementations optimize for the wrong one.
The deeper idea I keep returning to is skills as cognitive conversion. If you build a Claude skill around a strategic framework — say, Schumpeterian competition — and apply it repeatedly to real scenarios, something happens. You don’t just use the framework. You start thinking in it. The repetition changes your thought process. That’s conversion in the real sense: knowledge that becomes part of how you see the world, not just something you can recite.
I built a 411 case skill around this principle — a structured consulting engagement that walks through problem framing, solution development, and assumption mapping. The goal isn’t to complete the case. It’s to internalize the logic of how to approach one.
Efficiency should be rewarded, not punished. AI automates routine work, which should free time for conceptual thinking. Instead of 3 hours of formatting and synthesis, you need one really good idea. Education that penalizes AI use is education that mistakes the output for the point.

title: Interests & Exploration
text:
Implementation Ideas I’m Thinking About
These are speculative — directional, not prescriptive.
School-pays-for-compute. What if institutions purchased compute directly, and students accessed AI through school-managed accounts tied to coursework? You’d keep AI use in scope, track what’s being worked on, and remove cost as a barrier — without surrendering control to personal accounts where institutional knowledge leaks out.
Group skills. Multiple students interacting with the same AI skill simultaneously, where the conversation doesn’t advance until everyone responds. Forces collective reasoning. Mirrors how good teams actually work.
A prerequisite “Claude Skills” class. Before students use AI in domain-specific courses, they should understand how to build and evaluate the tools they’re using. Not coding — the logic of how skills are structured, what makes a prompt work, and how to tell when AI output should be trusted.
Assessment redesign. If skills are the unit of work, exams should test skill quality — not just output. Did you build a good process? Does your skill follow the right logic? The question shifts from “did you get the right answer” to “did you ask the right questions.”

title: Interests & Exploration
text:
Open Questions

How do you measure conversion (true learning) vs. surface-level AI-assisted output? The best measure I’ve found: length of time between studying and accurate recall, plus depth of application. But that’s hard to assess in a semester.
What level of technical depth is actually required for a non-technical student to leverage AI effectively? At what point does more knowledge stop returning value?
How do we preserve collaboration in an AI-augmented classroom — when AI can do individually what used to require a team?

title: Claude Code Skills
text:
Claude Code skills are reusable instruction sets that extend what Claude can do. Think of them as specialized modes — each one teaches Claude how to approach a specific task. Invoke /skill-name and Claude operates with that set of knowledge, constraints, and steps.
This page distinguishes between skills I built from scratch and tools I use heavily (with credit to their authors — that context matters).

title: Claude Code Skills
text:
Skills I Built
These are original skills I designed and wrote:
411 Case — Interactive business case analysis agent based on the 411 course methodology (David J. Bryce, BYU Marriott). Guides through a structured consulting engagement: problem framing → solution development → assumption mapping → recommendation.
Email Tracker — Reads AI/Claude Code newsletter emails via Gmail MCP, summarizes findings, researches links, and proposes updates to Claude setup files. Automated research pipeline, not just a reader.
Autoresearch — Self-improving skill optimizer. Runs a skill against test prompts, grades outputs against KPIs (phase-level + holistic), and iteratively improves the skill prompt. Inspired by Andrej Karpathy’s autoresearch methodology — I adapted it specifically for Claude Code skill optimization.
Dung — Parallel worktree orchestrator. Reads a project roadmap, identifies tasks that can run in parallel via dependency resolution, creates git worktrees, and outputs ready-to-run terminal commands. Named for the workhorse it is.
Close Session — End-of-session capture routine. 6 phases: project inventory → decisions made → memory updates → git cleanup → summary. Human-gated at every step. Prevents context loss between conversations.
Locker Router — Thin router that fetches skills from a remote Locker server. One skill that gives Claude access to all team skills stored server-side — the last mile of Locker’s delivery system.
KPI Setter — Maps any multi-step process into phases, defines binary KPIs per phase, and writes test scenarios. Pipeline: /kpi-setter → kpi.md → /autoresearch.
Brain Dump — Processes raw thoughts into organized memory files. For when you have 10 minutes and a lot in your head.

title: Claude Code Skills
text:
Tools I Use Heavily
Credit where it’s due. These tools shaped how I work day to day.

gstack (by Garry Tan)
A suite of skills for shipping software. I use these constantly:

/browse — Persistent headless Chromium, ~100ms per command. Navigate, click, screenshot, fill forms, check console errors.
/qa — Find bugs, fix with atomic commits, re-verify with screenshots. Loop until clean.
/ship — Fully automated release: merge base branch, run tests, audit diff, bump version, update CHANGELOG, commit, push, create PR.
/review — Pre-landing PR review. Pass 1: critical issues. Pass 2: informational. Auto-fixes obvious problems.
/plan-ceo-review — Founder mode: challenge the scope, find the 10-star product, expand or cut with conviction.
/plan-eng-review — Eng manager mode: lock in architecture, diagrams, edge cases, test coverage.
/qa-design-review — Visual audit. Grades A-F. Detects AI slop patterns, spacing issues, hierarchy problems. Auto-fixes iteratively.

gstack is the backbone of how I ship. The /browse + /qa + /ship loop is something I run on every project.


Karpathy’s Autoresearch
Andrej Karpathy published an autoresearch methodology: use AI to iteratively improve its own research and reasoning processes. His framing — run the process, grade the output, feed the grade back in, repeat — became the conceptual foundation for my own autoresearch skill, which applies that loop specifically to Claude Code skill optimization. The /kpi-setter → /autoresearch pipeline is a direct descendant of his work.


Anthropic’s Official Skills
Skills from Anthropic’s skills repository that I’ve installed and use regularly: /pdf, /pptx, /docx, /mcp-builder, /doc-coauthoring, /skill-creator, and others.

title: Claude Code Skills
text:

title: About Me
text:
What I Do
I build AI infrastructure — middleware, context systems, developer tooling. Currently working on Locker, enterprise skill middleware that lets teams share AI knowledge securely, and ContextOS, a context engineering platform that makes AI actually work for a whole company. I’ve also built 40+ Claude Code skills spanning research, shipping, QA, and knowledge capture.

title: About Me
text:
Background
BYU Marriott School of Business, Strategic Management (Class of 2028). Before going deep on AI, I worked in investment banking (Vermilion Rock Advisors), private equity (Ironclad Growth Partners), and valuation (Colliers International). That experience taught me how firms think about competitive advantage — now I build tools that create it.
This summer I’m joining BYU Sandbox, a venture-scale startup program, to take a product from idea to launch.

title: ContextOS
text:
The bottleneck to AI adoption isn’t the AI. It’s the context.
Companies say “everyone use AI” then wonder why it doesn’t work. It doesn’t work because the AI has zero knowledge of how the company operates — its processes, terminology, SOPs, and standards. 95% of GenAI pilots fail (MIT). 42% of companies abandoned most AI initiatives in 2025 (S&P Global).

title: ContextOS
text:
The bottleneck to AI adoption isn’t the AI. It’s the context.
Companies say “everyone use AI” then wonder why it doesn’t work. It doesn’t work because the AI has zero knowledge of how the company operates — its processes, terminology, SOPs, and standards. 95% of GenAI pilots fail (MIT). 42% of companies abandoned most AI initiatives in 2025 (S&P Global).

title: ContextOS
section: Three Problems with Enterprise AI Today
text:
Three Problems with Enterprise AI Today
1. No context. AI doesn’t know how your company works. Every employee must individually figure out prompting, what context to provide, and how to get useful output. The company should be doing that work, not each person.
2. Context burden on employees. Firms expect all employees to become AI professionals, rather than building the systems themselves — the structures, the security, the shared knowledge — so AI just works for everyone.
3. Knowledge spillover. When employees do connect company data to AI, they hand it to someone else’s LLM. A company’s competitive advantage lives in its processes (resource-based view, Barney 1991). When employees paste SOPs and workflows into personal AI accounts, those processes — the moats — become someone else’s training data. 90% of employees use personal AI accounts for work (Menlo Ventures).

title: ContextOS
text:
Why Now
Models are commoditizing. Open source is catching up fast. Compute costs are dropping. The model stops being the differentiator — firm context becomes the moat.
If you’re going to run models on your own infrastructure eventually, you still need structured firm context. ContextOS is model-agnostic.

title: ContextOS
section: The Research Case for Context Delivery
text:
The Research Case for Context Delivery
Companies default to fine-tuning when the research says: structure and deliver context at runtime.

RAG vs fine-tuning accuracy: 77-81% vs 20-30% on the same benchmarks (2-4x better)
Cost: RAG is 40% cheaper in year 1, with instant updates vs $500-$5K per retrain cycle
Market: RAG market projected to grow from $2.33B → $81.5B by 2035 (42.7% CAGR)

title: ContextOS
text:
Relationship to Locker
Locker secures and delivers your firm context to AI. ContextOS is where you build and structure that context. Eventually they merge: Locker’s auth, encryption, and MCP delivery infrastructure carries over — ContextOS replaces the storage and organization layer.

title: ContextOS
text:
Status
Research and design phase. Thesis articulated. Competitive landscape mapped. Data model defined. MVP scope: Upload → auto-organize → browse → MCP delivery.

title: Locker
text: Locker is enterprise middleware that sits between your team and their AI tools. It intercepts LLM calls, injects firm-specific skills on demand, and keeps proprietary content secure — so institutional knowledge travels with every AI interaction, automatically.

title: Locker
text: Locker is enterprise middleware that sits between your team and their AI tools. It intercepts LLM calls, injects firm-specific skills on demand, and keeps proprietary content secure — so institutional knowledge travels with every AI interaction, automatically.

title: Locker
text:
The Problem
Every developer building with AI writes their own prompts and workflows from scratch. There’s no shared layer. When someone on your team figures out the right way to do a code review, a spec write-up, or a client brief with AI — that knowledge lives in their head, not the organization’s.
Locker fixes that. Skills are deployed once and used by everyone, with visibility controls that let admins decide what employees can see.

title: Locker
text:
How It Works

Backend: FastAPI on Railway — Anthropic proxy, skill CRUD, team management, invite system
Frontend: Next.js dashboard on Vercel — unified admin UI + employee invite flow
Storage: PostgreSQL with AES-256 encryption at rest
Auth: Google OAuth sign-in; OAuth 2.1 for MCP; per-user Personal Access Tokens
CLI: locker deploy, locker sync, locker setup
MCP: SSE endpoint at /mcp + OAuth-protected /mcp/v2 — works natively with Claude Code

Skill visibility levels:



Level
What employees see




open
Full skill content


shared
Name and description only


hidden
Nothing — auto-injected silently

title: Locker
text:
Status
Phase 3. 240+ tests passing. Shipped: invite codes, personal access tokens, organization hierarchy, cross-team skill sharing, usage tracking, structured feedback, and OAuth 2.1.
